topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed a monotonous tune, a soundtrack to my escalating anxiety. Every time I looked up, my gaze snagged on her across the study tables—Elara. Her laughter, a bright, unexpected chime, seemed to vibrate in the quiet air, and my palms grew slick. I found myself re-reading the same passage in my textbook, not absorbing the words, but cataloging the way the light caught the curve of her neck as she turned a page. The news about the campus closure had been whispered in hushed tones, a slow-motion disaster unfolding around us, but it didn't diminish the way my stomach clenched whenever she happened to glance in my direction. I started taking unnecessary detours to the coffee shop near the quad, just to be in the vicinity of her presence, my heart thumping a frantic rhythm against my ribs that felt entirely out of control.

[story 2]

"Are you coming to the orientation mixer?" Liam asked, his voice casual, but the way he leaned in, his eyes lingering on my face for just a second too long, sent a dizzying rush through me. I nodded, trying to keep my expression neutral, but I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with the evening air. The reality of the university shutting down was a heavy blanket draped over everything, making the prospect of anything normal feel fragile. Yet, when Liam smiled, a genuine, unguarded expression, the world narrowed down to that moment. I found myself rehearsing conversations in my head, crafting the perfect witty response, desperate to catch his eye and make him see something more than just a classmate. Every shared glance felt like a secret code, a silent acknowledgment of a connection I desperately craved, especially as the looming deadline for final paperwork loomed closer.

[story 3]

I walked through the empty lecture halls, the silence amplifying the hollow echo of the impending end. It was suffocating. Then, she appeared by the exit, carrying a stack of worn poetry books. The way her hair caught the afternoon sun, the slight tilt of her head as she adjusted the books—it was mesmerizing. I stopped walking, my breath catching somewhere deep in my chest. My usual practiced composure dissolved; I felt clumsy, suddenly aware of the way my shirt felt against my skin. When she finally looked up, meeting my gaze, there was a spark, a recognition that made my entire frame feel charged. I managed a small, shaky smile, my voice coming out breathier than I intended. "That's a beautiful selection," I managed, the words feeling inadequate against the sudden, overwhelming pull I felt toward her presence.
